replies.title: Re: Helios Unbound for the Dolmen Arch path
replies.content:
In addition to carefully reading the Abramelin document, you might also want to look at William Bloom's published account of his own Abramelin working and the unexpected (beneficial, I think, on the whole) changes that it producd in him: The Sacred Magician: A Ceremonial Diary.

There are two editions, published by Paladin in 1976 and by Gothic Image in 1992. The earlier edition was published pseudonymously (as if by "Georges Chevalier"), the later under the author's own name. Each includes important material that the author thought it best not to publish at the time of the other edition, so ideally one might want to read both editions side by side.

Note, too, that the first English publication of the Abramelin text, by S. L. MacGregor Mathers, was made from a single, very incomplete French manuscript translation, which omitted the entire fourth book of the treatise. In the last several decades much fuller and better editions have appeared in the original German, by Jürg von Ins (1988) and by Geog Dehn (2001). Dehn's edition has also been published in English translation (2006).

The full Abramelin working takes many months to complete (6 months, IIRC), and requires the magician to be engaged with it pretty much 24/7. It strikes me as a working not to be undertaken lightly, much less to be altered to suit the worker's personal taste. When Bloom did it, it upset much of what he had valued in his previous life, though he thought the changes it had produced were on the whole beneficial to him. Among other things, it wholly changed the nature of the relationship he had wih his very supportive significant other. IIRC, among other things, it made that relationship much less sexual, or perhaps not sexual at all any longer. (Bloom is properly reticent about the details.)

replies.content: For some ideas from an engineering perspective of how this could be implemented while maintaining a degree of independence sufficient to avoid paradoxes between two lives, when the two lives are separated by a series of ordinary worldly relationships over space, see the literature on "exact Markov-chain Monte Carlo sampling" and "coupling from the past", which is about engineering perfect independence between two statistical samples, when the two samples are separated by a series of steps of being altered to other plausible statistical samples over time. This might require the details of the two lives and the surrounding world to get "roughed in" incrementally in parallel, but there's some reason to believe that something like that were happening anyway. (Of course, in the "final draft" of the world, everyone would have memories that were consistent the same way any other details of the world would be.) The extra work needed to set this up might also result in some added expense of some sort compared to ordinary sequential incarnation, which would be why it would be less common.

comments.title: What's happening here?
comments.content:
I have a menagerie of interesting acquaintances. One’s a clairvoyant and all-around psychic. When we first met we had quite a conversation in which I thought my thoughts and the clairvoyant responded out loud. It went on for some time. Another person I know can see the shining of the presence of the Shekinah. Normally the presence shines from the consecrated host at mass. Sometimes the presence shines from the priest when he’s reading the gospel or delivering the homily. Sometimes the presence shines from a reader, or outside of mass from speakers at religious gatherings.

My question is about a third person. First some background about this person. The person has no personal ritual ceremonial practice, nor any set of personal psychic practices, nor any set of traditional spiritual practices of any sort. Nor is the person outwardly religious, nor a churchgoer. The person is not psychic nor clairvoyant in any way. Nor does the person possess any special gifts, like being able to see the Shekinah. The person cares for and saves the lives of animals, insects, bugs, trees, and plants, and even prays for them when they pass away (including the bugs and plants). The person comes across to other people as being very kind. But otherwise the person is quite withdrawn and suffers from Complex-PTSD due to long term familial abuse starting in childhood.

Here’s the thing. The clairvoyant cannot bear to look directly at this person’s aura. The clairvoyant once pointed to the sun when it was on the horizon and said that this person’s aura was like that. The clairvoyant also says that this person has a very strong shield that’s always there. In one incident, the clairvoyant attempted to exorcise a demon that had taken possession of a religious icon. The clairvoyant failed and a larger more terrifying demon appeared instead. The clairvoyant fled and came to the person in question, who prayed for a holy angel to come and protect them. What happened said the clairvoyant was that this person’s shield simply expanded and drove the demon back.

My acquaintance who can see the presence of the Shekinah says that this person in question is the only one from whom the Shekinah has shone when the person was not doing anything religious in particular. Not reading from a sacred text, nor performing any religious act, nor hanging out in any religious place, but just silently sitting at table in the house.

What might be happening with this person? Are past lives involved? I ask in the general sense. In other words, when this kind of thing happens with a person, what in general is happening? I also invite insights from the commentariat. Neither the clairvoyant nor the person gifted to see the Shekinah have any idea what’s happening.

Obsidian Melancholic Mermaid

I would encourage you and others to make sure this holy person has whatever they need, give them the solitude they require, and give thanks to the gods that someone of that stature is in the area -- such a person becomes a center of beneficial influences that can extend quite far.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Arbatel isn't PGM-style magic. That's like asking if a rowboat is a good first bicycle.

I'd encourage you to start with thorough study of the PGM documents, backed up with scholarly works such as Garth Fowden's The Egyptian Hermes and your choice of modern works riffing off the PGM rituals. Then choose a ritual and work with it systematically. My recommendation for a first working is always the Bornless One ritual, as it's been used so systematically of late that the tracks in space are very solid, but your mileage may vary, of course.

If you want to study the Arbatel and then cross-reference it with the PGM, that's fine, and might well be productive -- but I'd encourage you to get a good background in the original tradition first.

comments.title: German Texans, Heart/Solar Plexus Adjustments, and Ritual for the Honored Dead
comments.content:
Hello All,

First off, I wish everyone the best with however you do (or don't) observe Samhain/Halloween/Dia de los Muertos/mid-Fall. One of the few good things to come out of the COVID lockdowns around here is that our neighborhood started setting up tables outside to hand out candy for trick-or-treating, which pretty naturally turned into a tailgating/block party kind of event - parents sip on adult beverages, share snacks with the other grown-ups, and chat while the kids go for the candy. So, however you observe the season, may your community of the living and the dead bring you good things this year.

Secondly, thanks to JMG for providing this space and to the community for making it so lively.

To Share: Last week, fellow commenter causticus put out a thoughtful post (https://causticus.dreamwidth.org/66328.html) on how folks who consider themselves "Germanic" polytheists, especially Americans, have a wider set of influences to draw on than many folks look to. This led me to start looking further into Texas's history of substantial German settlement - a topic I was pretty familiar with, but had never considered from a religious point of view. So far I haven't found much, but I'll share as I do. If anyone would like to do their own poking around, the Wikipedia entry on "German Texan" has some good links to get you started: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_Texan

To Ask: Two questions if I might this week:
1) I've seen you (JMG) in past comments talk about how the Middle Pillar has some flexibility in using the heart center or the solar plexus, and that a person ought to use the one that they most need to develop (solar plexus if you could use more strength of will, heart if you could use more feeling/compassion). Is there any equivalent tweaking in the DMH/DA approach, or does the balance between Solar and Telluric forces make it not as necessary?
2) For JMG and/or the commentariat: any recommendations for family-friendly and flexible ways to observe the holiday without the chanting and walking in circles of a non-magical Grove ritual? My wife is more toward the "spiritual but not religious" end of things, but she asked me about doing something in particular to honor and remember our dead (she's from San Antonio, so Dia de los Muertos is familiar, if not directly part of her cultural tradition). If it helps, I usually light a candle to my "forebearers" (both blood ancestors and important cultural figures) and offer a drink once a week, and I pray to the Anglo-Saxon Gods.

Many thanks as usual to JMG and the commentariat for one of the highlights of my week.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.content:

2) You can always have a feast, set an extra place for the dead, and say some prayers, then spend the evening looking over family pictures if you have them and talking about memories.

comments.title: Spiritual Evolution of Trees

Hi JMG,

First of all, wishing a blessed Samhain for you and yours, and for all those who are marking the season this year!

A few topics of inquiry for this week:

1. I am curious about the spiritual evolution of plants and trees. I recall you mentioning that trees were on their own parallel path of evolution, but also that they were the point of transition between other plants and animal life, in terms of incarnation.

This leaves me wondering: are there beings similar to plants, trees, or indeed fungus in the higher planes? And what could the evolutionary path of those beings be? Could some plants or mushrooms 'skip' the animal phase of evolution entirely?

According to Russian fitness coach Pavel Tsatsouline, Russian weight-lifters will refuse to step over their gym equipment ("if you step over barbell, you get beaten up and thrown out of gym", he said in an amusing exchange with Joe Rogan). Luke

replies.title: Re: Spiritual Evolution of Trees

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you much for the Simpson recommendation, that sounds fantastic!

If you want to go really intense on the Icelandic angle, you can get the complete Sagas of the Icelanders from here: https://sagas.is/vara.php . Somewhat self-consciously literary, but also the main "primary sources" we have for the settlement and medieval period of Iceland.

Maybe too close to the tales of the Brothers Grimm, as it was consciously inspired by them, but The Turnip Princess is a collection of German folk/fairy tales collected by a contemporary of theirs that languished in a town's archive until the early 2000's. Lots of the same fairy tale motifs as the more familiar Grimm stories, but not as "polished".

Speaking of the Brothers Grimm, their Teutonic Mythology focuses a lot more on the nitty gritty of folk practices and details from the stories that might derive from older Heathen practice than their "Marchen". You can find various versionson archive.org or Google Books, but the different editions and volumes can get a bit confusing. I was happy to get some printed copies from Cambridge press.

I can't speak to the quality of the stories, as I haven't read it yet, but Nordic Tales with illustrations by Ulla Thynell collects an assortment of 19th and early 20th century fairy/folk tales form around Scandinavia (including Finland) with some pleasant pastel pictures. In the back it lays out the sources, which might be more what you're looking for.

Unfortunately, I don't yet have anything of the sort I can recommend on personal experience for more local tales. I have my eye on Texas Indian Myths and Legends by Jane Arcger, and I hope it's the kind of thing you're talking about.

I also look forward to seeing what others might share!

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.content:
I'm increasingly coming into contact with people who want to be referred to by other pronouns than he or her. I'm trying to form an opinion on the topic, and I'm asking here because the occult view on the topic seems to help resolve the issue, as I outline below.

On one hand, if someone identifies with a different gender than what they have been born with, then that sounds like it would cause them a great amount of emotional and social difficulty, which is hard for me to fully understand because I'm not faced with this situation myself.

On the other hand, there is this popular idea that gender can and should be separate from the material limits of whatever equipment your body was born with. That you have the ability to choose your reality regardless of whatever limits you've been dealt with.

The way to resolve the binary could be the awareness that, as discussed here before, there are lots of souls waiting to come back into incarnation and they haven't processed their past lives in a different gender, which may explain why people don't feel at home in their biological sex. If this is the case, then those faced with such a situation would deserve more sympathy than they do from some political commentators on the right currently give them. Although using the word 'sympathy' makes it sound like a disorder or a flaw, which I don't want to convey as I'm not sure if it's the case.

Although maybe I'm conflating the issue of gender dysphoria with how people have chosen to handle it by using additional pronouns. Doing so does seem to have the advantage that it conveys neutrality in terms of gender when referring to someone.

Apologies to any readers if I am writing insensitively about the issue, I am just trying to understand it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You've written about this as though it were entirely a question of real gender identity issues and honoring each individual's experience. While that is one worthwhile lens through which to view the issue, you might also want to contemplate it from the perspective of power plays, manipulations, and moonshots in the Olympics of the aggrieved. Some people are actually struggling with identity issues, but some are simply LARPing the highest status-conferring role they've been able to parse out from the incredibly intricate hierarchies governing the distribution of Woke privileges.

In the bizarre, navel-gazing world of Woke, pretending gender dysphoria entitles any competent performers of that drag style to engage in all kinds of misbehaviors and to enjoy unearned privileges over their competitors. Those laurels of glory will eventually be seized by an even more rarefied and costly identification, just as they have already slipped from the clutches of wymyn, gays, blacks (lives mattered), and Latinxes. Fortunately, when transgenderism is no longer the reigning queen in the internecine holy war raging across Wokedom, the number of trans LARPers will fall precipitously, allowing the honestly gender dysphoric to breathe a much deserved sigh of relief.

As for pronouns and other labels, our trigger-hair sensitivity to perceived slights is often directly proportional to both our own sense of unworthiness and our aspirations to unearned status. Therefore, those who can't handle being referred to by the long-term gender conventions of their common language might find that the internal tensions they experience from others' speech could be more easily resolved by internal work than by trying to exert control over the universe around them. None of us can control the universe. Heck, most of us don't even have much control over ourselves yet.

Personally, I have found no end of pleasure in learning to embrace all the intentionally belittling and degrading epithets for gay man that have been hurled at me. It's amazing how one's status and sense of self worth can soar merely by responding, "You're damn straight I'm a blankety blank blank, and awfully proud of it. Whatever could have clued you into that oh so subtle insight, my dear?" Perhaps the greater trans community could achieve no end of healing by rediscovering its lost sense of humor? I rather fondly remember when it had one.

replies.content:
One thing you may also consider is that Western culture tends to rely on external mass-market culture as a stand-in for personally engaging with the world. "Gender dysphoria" carries with it the loaded assumption that the same mass market that promotes processed foods, cable news, and cheap plastic big box store junk can provide gender roles that are healthy and satisfying for most people.

Another thing that strikes me is that it seems relatively straightforward to experiment with pronouns and such in the privacy of personal meditation. It may be enlightening to, for instance, imagine what it would be like to assign yourself the opposite gender and see what the world looks like from there.

Lastly, you might ask yourself what material limits you're talking about. I recall a self-help exercise of taking a few things you think are impossible and believing that you can actually do them. Inevitably, you will find things that can't just be believed away. However, you'll also find we tend to make a lot of unnecessary self-defeating assumptions that make us miserable, and even many material limits can be worked around to get us some of what we need. If all someone has known is pop culture gender roles that don't fit right, it can make sense to kick at them a bit to see if they can come up with something that fits better.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My take on this is unpopular on both sides of the culture wars. As I see it, gender dysphoria is unquestionably a real thing, and it has at least two roots, one metaphysical, the other cultural. The metaphysical root is the fact that so many souls are returning to incarnation too soon after their previous death, with memories of their previous body not yet processed. The cultural root is that both men and women in our society are supposed to conform to toxic, self-contradictory gender roles, and it's quite common for people raised in one such role to end up convincing themselves that everything would be fine if only they adopted the other role. Since nobody's supposed to think about questioning the roles, and defining their own way to be male or female, that's the one escape hatch left; it's a fake exit, of course, but that's quite common in our society these days.

The pronoun thing is partly a reflection of this, partly an opportunity to bully other people into conformity, and partly a form of fake rebelliousness that people are encouraged to take up as a distraction to keep them from doing anything more productive. It seems rather silly to me, but then I've never had much patience with mindless conformity to cultural fads. Nobody's yet asked me for my pronouns; if it happens, I may just explain that I identify as an Elder God and my pronouns are "Cthulhu" and "Fhtagn."

comments.title: Alan Wallace Meditation
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I posted a couple weeks ago asking about meditation tips for my 11 year old son, and you and another poster suggested Alan Wallace's Shamatha meditation book on Buddhist meditation.

I've started reading it and have begun to apply it for my own meditation before moving my son into it and I had some followup questions:

1. I have seen you post many times about how mindfulness meditation (as it is mostly taught in the West) can have negative consequences and can effectively be a non-chemical tranquilizer for the brain to help it ignore the condition of society etc. You've also said that this doesn't apply when practiced in its native context (by Buddhist monks etc) because then it is done in the context of a broader complete framework including other practices that prevent this issue.

Now in the book, Wallace is constantly talking about how, in order to meditate one must guard against the *twin* dangers of "excitation" and "laxity". He says something like how excitation (basically distraction and lots of thoughts jumping around in the brain) is the first thing most people have to deal with, but "laxity" is just as big a problem to guard against. He calls laxity a kind of "dullness" or "lethargy" of the brain where the brain kind of sinks down and goes blank and some people think that the purpose of meditation is to reach this state of no-thought and blankness, but actually it's really dangerous and he mentions he knew someone in the 1970s who went too far down this path of making his mind blank and essentially turned himself into a vegetable. He is constantly warning how meditation means balancing between the twin dangers and how it actually means having focussed attention on whatever the subject of the meditation is and not jumping around too much (excitation) or totally blank and dull (laxity).

Anyway, my question is - am I correct in thinking that he is essentially warning against the same dangers you are warning against when talking about the risks of mindfulness meditation? It seems that the advice he is giving in the book *is* the advice given to those practicing a complete system of Buddhist meditation to avoid this problem? I have to say, other than your writings, this is the only place where I've heard of the risks of "laxity" - HeadSpace, Calm, and meditation apps like that, or any meditation advice I've ever read - never mentions this aspect at all.

2. In the book, Wallace sets out 10 stages of Shamatha meditation that one needs to go through to finally achieve Shamatha as the 10th stage. He says this is a long process that will take thousands of hours of practice and dedication etc and all of that makes sense.

But what I wanted to ask is - he is basically saying that even to reach Stage 3/4, you pretty much need to be retreating from society and reshaping your life and spending several hours each day in meditation. Is that true?

I know Buddhist Shamatha meditation is not quite the same as the discursive meditation you practice, but that really jumped out at me. Do I really need to become a full time meditator to get beyond even Stage 3? I can understand if one needs to do that to get to Stage 7/8/9/10 - but Stage 3?

Or let me rephrase my question - I'm not really asking you to comment on the specifics of Shamatha meditation and Wallace's system - I'm more asking - is it true that to go beyond the beginner stages and even touch the intermediate levels briefly (much less become a high level meditator), one has to retreat from society and become essentially a full-time meditator? I just find that hard to accept..

replies.title: Re: Alan Wallace Meditation
replies.content:
one has to retreat from society and become essentially a full-time meditator? I just find that hard to accept..

Each school of Buddhism has its own rule set. So take the following with a grain of salt.

The monastic practice allows a casting off of distractions that can help grow and aspect of understanding of the Dharma. However as its pointed out by the "Engaged Buddhists" that the point of enlightened practice is - in part - to help alleviate suffering in the "real world"

No post on the internet can fully answer this ongoing debate. If the school has writings touching on the practice of Buddhist Ethics I would think it would be well worth studying in addition to the meditation practice.

replies.title: Re: Alan Wallace Meditation
replies.content:
I'm the one who recommended the Alan Wallace book.

1. Your assessment of this is correct. To be extremely blunt, most of what passes for Buddhist teaching in the west is a flaming dumpster fire at this point.

2. I'm really not sure what led him to make those claims. It may be he was quoting from an old manual. If memory serves, that and his claim that Shamatha doesn't mix well with vigorous exercise were my only two problems with the text. I know three people who were able to reach stage 3 or 4 practicing an hour or two daily. One of them, who reached stage four; was leading a lifestyle that traditional Buddhist teaching would regard as deeply unconducive to spiritual advancement.

Your rephrased question is a lot easier to answer. If you want to reach the highest humanly possible levels of meditative skill, becoming a monk and meditating full time is probably a good way to do that. Please notice that I said a good way, not the only way. The awareness of the body and breathing can have profound health impacts; which is highly beneficial to martial arts. The mental focus of stage three starts to release phenomena that most people would regard as superhuman. This isn't expanded on in the text though, because the focus is on training the attention, not applying meditation to martial arts or the practice of east asian magic.

To misquote our esteemed host: if you don't want to become a monk and retreat from society, then go ye forth and don't do the thing. :D

replies.title: Re: Alan Wallace Meditation
replies.content:
Thank you for this. It’s super useful (I’m the OP). As I think I mentioned a couple weeks ago, my son is very into boxing, but I should have added he gets it from me and I have a background in both boxing and Japanese martial arts (aikido), albeit the latter with zero esoteric or magical practices.

As a result I would love any tips you have on where I can learn more or read about applying meditation to martial arts for myself as well as for my son - I tried googling but to use your phrase, the results are a “dumpster fire”.

PS - It is interesting - I have tried discursive meditation before, and also the Headspace type of stuff and the stuff Wallace is talking about just instinctively appeals to me way more even before I knew about the martial aspects of it, so I do want to see how far I can get with it, while doing a job and being a family man and not retreating to the mountains…

replies.title: Re: Alan Wallace Meditation
replies.content:
It is a delight to hear that you got the book and that it has been of use to you.

Your best bet is to learn about meditation, learn about martial arts, then work on putting them together. Once you understand that the relationship is more likely to be implied or insinuated rather than openly discussed, relevant material starts to show up in some rather odd places. Worth noting; right meditation and right action are usually put on opposite sides of the dharma wheel.

The martial arts classics are probably your next best bet. I really wish I had some more concrete directions to give.

In regards to your post script, large portions of the writings on Bushido were created for Buddhist laymen (householders) who weren't willing to allow becoming a monk to interfere with their mundane duties.

replies.title: Re: Alan Wallace Meditation
replies.content:
I'm not a specialist in Shamatha meditation. I'm not qualified to comment on Wallace's system, because it's not just that Shamatha medittion is "not quite the same as" discursive meditation. It's a completely different system, as different as swimming is from soccer.

I know that discursive meditation can be practiced intensively every day for years, and yield impressive benefits and potent spiritual transformations, without interfering with one's capacity to engage in everyday life. I know that there are other forms of meditation for which that isn't true. If you want to know about the fine details of Shamatha meditation, however, please talk to someone who has experience with it!

comments.title: Viktor Schauberger
comments.content:
Greetings! Thank you JMG, once again for hosting this space!

Looking into the more esoteric realms of agriculture, I found Viktor Schauberger, an Austrian forest keeper and inventor, alive around the time of the WWII.

Regarding agriculture, he talks about two things that I found really interesting, and birthed the questions for this Monday:

1) using steel tools destroys earth's fertility. He explains this by way of rust particles that are generated when using such tools, and that those interfere with the way water and land behaves. When reading about it, I reminded how steel is used to drive away fae folk, and wondered about weather the steel could be driving away elementals or other non-corporeal folk that take part in the unseen part nature's work. Does this make sense? If this is the case, is there a way to warn the iron sensitive beings, or some way to reduce the impact of using such tools? Is there anyone in the commentariat that has experience in using non-iron tools to work the land?

2) he also talks about agriculture as a way to bring together the energies from the land and sky, and that without trees the land's energy gets messed up/weak. He suggests a way to remedy this, by burying a 2m tall egg of clay, filled with water, copper and zinc shavings, together with some manure. The water is then stirred with a paddle made of wood and with some cooper and silver in it. The egg has a small hole that is then covered with a linen cloth. I wonder if anyone had practical experience with such apparatus? I searched online but couldn't find anything. Or are there any other method suggested to bring back fertility? I'm now looking at bio dynamic farming, but any pointers would be appreciated.

Kind wishes,
Fox

replies.title: Re: Viktor Schauberger
replies.content:
Schauberger was a very, very strange cat. His methods work; his theories range from the interesting to the batshale crazy -- but his methods work.

1) Elementals aren't bothered by iron, but there are spiritual beings that are. Iron and steel also discharge certain kinds of etheric energy -- that's why steel swords, daggers, and tridents are used by ceremonial magicians. I don't think there's a way to lessen that effect aside from using nonferrous tools. IIRC there are suppliers in Europe, but I can't find them online either -- it interests me that search engines increasingly bring up irrelevant sites.

2) There are several biodynamic preparations that have similar effects, and some ends of the radionics community have methods as well. Why not give Schauberger's device a try, though, and see how it works?

replies.content:

Not sure about the shipping situation if you're outside of Europe/UK though.

how should I interpret rene guenon?
I have difficulties. If this doesn't go here, delete the comment, thanks

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a perfectly valid question for this open post. It's also timely, as I'm rereading The Reign of Quantity right now!

How to interpret René Guenon? That's challenging, because he combines genuine and very deep esoteric knowledge with a degree of snotnosed arrogance and egotistical posturing that makes Aleister Crowley look meek and humble. He created his own personal synthesis of occult teaching, slapped the label "Tradition" on it, and proceeded to swan around insisting that he had the One True Eternal Teaching and everyone who disagreed with him was either an ignorant fool or a sinister agent of the Counter-Tradition. That's unfortunate, because there's a lot of value in his writing -- it's just necessary to ignore his personality to get to his useful insights.

One of these days I need to do an essay on what probably has to be called the Traditionalist deviation -- the mistaken belief that older ideas are by definition better than newer ones, the flipside of the Progressive deviation, which holds that newer ideas are by definition better than older ones. It's as though, to borrow a phrase of Yeats, both sides were convinced that the strawberries were refuted when over. Each phase of the historical process has its proper philosophy, and what seems right from one end of that process seems wrong from the other, but each is right in its own era.

replies.title: Re: Yeat's A Vision
replies.content: The 1937 edition is the one to get; Yeats commented in the foreword that he'd completely misunderstood some of the concepts and the 1925 version was badly flawed as a result. replies.title: Re: Geomancy reading
replies.content: A house chart for a yes or no question, as I read such things, is always interpreted on the basis of the presence or absence of a mode of perfection uniting the significators of the querent and the quesited. The 1st house always signifies the querent, and the house of the quesited is determined by the subject of the question; in this case, since it's a career question, the 10th house is the one that matters. Laetitia (in the 1st) and Puer (in the 10th) do not spring anywhere else in the chart, and no third figure unites them by translation. The answer is therefore "no."
